exercises.prompt: List every distinct primary type (type1), alphabetically.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT DISTINCT type1 FROM pokemon ORDER BY type1
hints: DISTINCT goes right after SELECT and removes duplicate rows.
Alphabetical means ORDER BY type1 (ascending is the default).

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT type1 FROM pokemon ORDER BY type1
```

exercises.prompt: How many distinct generations of Pokémon exist? Return a single number.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT generation) AS generations FROM pokemon
hints: You can put DISTINCT inside COUNT(...).
COUNT(DISTINCT generation) counts each generation value once.

exercises.prompt: How many distinct primary types (type1) appear across all Pokémon? Return a single number.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT type1) AS type_count FROM pokemon
hints: You want the count of unique type1 values, not the number of rows.
Put DISTINCT inside COUNT: COUNT(DISTINCT type1).

exercises.prompt: List every distinct primary type (type1) that at least one legendary Pokémon has, in alphabetical order.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT DISTINCT type1 FROM pokemon WHERE is_legendary ORDER BY type1
hints: Filter to legendary Pokémon first, then collapse duplicate types with DISTINCT.
SELECT DISTINCT type1 ... WHERE is_legendary, then ORDER BY type1.

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT type1 FROM pokemon WHERE is_legendary ORDER BY type1
```

exercises.prompt: List every distinct combination of primary and secondary type — one row per unique (type1, type2) pair. Show type1 and type2. Single-typed Pokémon have a NULL type2; include those combinations too.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT DISTINCT type1, type2 FROM pokemon
hints: DISTINCT applies to the whole selected row, so listing two columns yields unique pairs.
SELECT DISTINCT type1, type2 FROM pokemon — no filtering needed.

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT type1, type2 FROM pokemon
```

exercises.prompt: For legendary Pokémon only, list every distinct combination of generation and primary type (type1). Show generation and type1, ordered by generation ascending, then type1 ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT DISTINCT generation, type1 FROM pokemon WHERE is_legendary ORDER BY generation ASC, type1 ASC
hints: Filter to legendary Pokémon, take the unique generation/type1 pairs, then order by both columns.
SELECT DISTINCT generation, type1 ... WHERE is_legendary, then ORDER BY generation ASC, type1 ASC.

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT generation, type1 FROM pokemon WHERE is_legendary ORDER BY generation ASC, type1 ASC
```
